title: NPS (National Pension System) Guide 2026 — Tax Benefits, Returns, and Should You Invest?
meta_description: Complete NPS guide for India 2026. Learn about NPS tax benefits under 80CCD, returns (12-14% equity), Tier 1 vs Tier 2, fund managers, exit rules, and NPS vs PPF vs ELSS comparison.
content:
What Is NPS (National Pension System)?

The National Pension System (NPS) is a government-backed retirement savings scheme regulated by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA). It was launched in 2004 for government employees and opened to all Indian citizens in 2009.

Think of NPS as a long-term investment account where you put money regularly during your working years. The money gets invested in a mix of equity, bonds, and government securities. When you retire (at 60), you get a portion as a lump sum and the rest as a monthly pension.

NPS is one of the most tax-efficient retirement tools in India. You can claim up to Rs 2 lakh in tax deductions — Rs 1.5 lakh under Section 80CCD(1) and an additional Rs 50,000 under Section 80CCD(1B). No other investment gives you this double benefit.

As of 2026, NPS manages over Rs 13 lakh crore in assets for more than 7.5 crore subscribers. The equity component has delivered 12-14% annualised returns over the last 10 years, making it one of the best-performing retirement products in India.

How NPS Works — Tier 1 vs Tier 2 Accounts

NPS has two types of accounts. Understanding the difference is crucial before you invest.

Tier 1 Account (Mandatory Retirement Account)

This is the main NPS account. It is a long-term, locked-in retirement account with restricted withdrawals.

  • Minimum contribution: Rs 1,000 per year (to keep the account active)
  • Lock-in: Until age 60 (with limited partial withdrawal allowed after 3 years)
  • Tax benefits: Full tax deductions under 80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B)
  • At maturity (age 60): 60% can be withdrawn as a tax-free lump sum; 40% must be used to buy an annuity (monthly pension)
  • Partial withdrawal: Up to 25% of your own contributions after 3 years, for specific reasons (children's education, marriage, house purchase, medical treatment) — maximum 3 times during the entire tenure

Tier 2 Account (Voluntary Savings Account)

This is an optional, flexible investment account linked to your Tier 1 account.

  • Minimum contribution: Rs 250 per transaction
  • Lock-in: None — withdraw anytime (except for government employees claiming tax benefits, who have a 3-year lock-in)
  • Tax benefits: No tax deductions for most investors. Government employees get Section 80C deduction with a 3-year lock-in
  • Use case: Think of it as a mutual fund alternative with very low expense ratios (0.01-0.09%)
FeatureTier 1Tier 2
PurposeRetirement savings (mandatory)Voluntary savings (flexible)
Lock-in periodUntil age 60No lock-in
Tax benefitUp to Rs 2 lakh (80CCD)None (Govt. employees: 80C with 3-yr lock-in)
Minimum yearly contributionRs 1,000Rs 250 per transaction
WithdrawalPartial after 3 years (25%, max 3 times)Anytime, no limit
PrerequisiteNoneMust have Tier 1 account

Verdict: For most investors, Tier 1 is the primary account you should focus on. Tier 2 is useful only if you want a low-cost equity or debt fund with instant liquidity — but you lose the tax benefits.

Who Should Invest in NPS?

NPS is not for everyone. Here is a clear breakdown of who benefits the most.

1. Salaried Employees (Private Sector)

If you are in the 30% tax bracket (income above Rs 15 lakh under old regime), NPS is almost a no-brainer. The extra Rs 50,000 deduction under 80CCD(1B) saves you Rs 15,600 in taxes every year (including cess). Over 25 years with 12% returns, that Rs 50,000/year grows to over Rs 94 lakh.

Even if you are in the new tax regime, your employer's NPS contribution (up to 14% of basic salary for central government, 10% for others) is tax-free under Section 80CCD(2) — this works in BOTH old and new tax regimes.

2. Self-Employed Individuals

If you are a freelancer, business owner, or professional (doctor, lawyer, CA), NPS gives you the 80CCD(1B) deduction that is available over and above the Rs 1.5 lakh limit of Section 80C. This is especially valuable if you have already maxed out 80C with PPF, ELSS, or insurance premiums.

3. Government Employees

Since 2004, all new central government employees are automatically enrolled in NPS (replacing the old pension scheme). The government contributes 14% of basic + DA to your NPS account. This is essentially free money. State government employees in most states also get NPS.

4. People Who Want Forced Discipline

The lock-in until 60 is a feature, not a bug. If you tend to dip into your savings, NPS forces you to stay invested for the long term. This compounding over 25-30 years creates serious wealth.

Who Should NOT Invest in NPS?

  • Young investors (under 30) who want full flexibility: If you might need the money before 60, NPS is too restrictive. Consider ELSS (3-year lock-in) or PPF (15-year lock-in) instead.
  • People in the new tax regime (without employer contribution): If your employer does not contribute to NPS, you get zero tax benefit in the new regime. In this case, index funds or ELSS may be better.
  • NRIs: NPS rules for NRIs are complex. OCI holders are not eligible. NRIs can invest, but repatriation rules are restrictive.

NPS Tax Benefits — The Complete Breakdown

This is where NPS truly shines. No other investment product offers tax benefits at three levels: contribution, growth, and partial withdrawal.

Section 80CCD(1) — Employee's Own Contribution

  • Deduction limit: Up to 10% of salary (basic + DA) for salaried; up to 20% of gross income for self-employed
  • Maximum: Rs 1.5 lakh (this is part of the overall 80C limit of Rs 1.5 lakh, NOT additional)
  • Available in: Old tax regime only
  • Important: If you already claim Rs 1.5 lakh under 80C (PPF, ELSS, insurance, etc.), this section gives you no extra benefit

Section 80CCD(1B) — Additional NPS Deduction

  • Deduction limit: Rs 50,000 per year
  • This is OVER AND ABOVE the Rs 1.5 lakh limit of 80C
  • Available in: Old tax regime only
  • Tax saving: Rs 15,600 (30% bracket + 4% cess) to Rs 5,200 (5% bracket + 4% cess)
  • Who can claim: Any NPS subscriber (salaried or self-employed)

This is the single biggest reason people invest in NPS. An extra Rs 50,000 deduction that no other product offers.

Section 80CCD(2) — Employer's Contribution

  • Deduction limit: Up to 14% of basic + DA (central government) or 10% of basic + DA (private sector)
  • No upper cap (unlike 80C's Rs 1.5 lakh limit)
  • Available in: BOTH old and new tax regimes
  • How it works: Your employer contributes to your NPS account. This amount is tax-free for you and is a business expense for your employer.

This is the most powerful NPS tax benefit. If your basic salary is Rs 10 lakh, your employer can contribute Rs 1 lakh (10%) to NPS — completely tax-free. Many companies now offer this as part of the salary restructuring.

Tax at Maturity (Exit Taxation)

ComponentTax Treatment
60% lump sum withdrawalCompletely tax-free (since Budget 2024)
40% annuity purchaseTax-free at the time of purchase. Pension income is taxed as per your slab.
Premature exit (before 60)Only 20% can be withdrawn (taxed as income). Remaining 80% must buy an annuity.
Death of subscriber100% of corpus is paid to nominee — completely tax-free.

Total Tax Benefit Example (30% Bracket, Old Regime)

SectionAmountTax Saved
80CCD(1) — within 80C limitRs 1,50,000Rs 46,800
80CCD(1B) — additionalRs 50,000Rs 15,600
80CCD(2) — employer (10% of Rs 10L basic)Rs 1,00,000Rs 31,200
TotalRs 3,00,000Rs 93,600

That is nearly Rs 1 lakh saved in taxes every year. Over 25 years, the tax savings alone compound into a significant corpus.

NPS Asset Classes — Where Your Money Gets Invested

Unlike PPF or FD where the government decides the interest rate, NPS lets you choose how your money is invested across four asset classes.

Asset Class E — Equity

  • Invests in: Index funds tracking Nifty 50, Sensex, and other equity benchmarks
  • Risk: Highest among NPS options (but lower than direct equity or equity mutual funds)
  • Historical returns: 12-14% annualised over 10 years
  • Maximum allocation: 75% (until age 50), then reduces by 2.5% every year until it reaches 50% at age 60
  • Best for: Young investors (25-40) who can handle volatility

Asset Class C — Corporate Bonds

  • Invests in: Bonds issued by public sector companies, banks, and highly rated corporates
  • Risk: Moderate
  • Historical returns: 9-11% annualised over 10 years
  • Best for: Moderate risk appetite, provides stability with decent returns

Asset Class G — Government Securities

  • Invests in: Central and state government bonds, treasury bills
  • Risk: Lowest (sovereign guarantee)
  • Historical returns: 8-10% annualised over 10 years
  • Best for: Conservative investors, people close to retirement

Asset Class A — Alternative Investments

  • Invests in: REITs, InvITs, CMBS, and other alternative instruments
  • Risk: Moderate to high
  • Historical returns: Limited track record (introduced later)
  • Maximum allocation: 5% of total corpus
  • Best for: Diversification — adds a small allocation to real estate and infrastructure
Asset ClassWhat It Invests InRisk Level10-Year ReturnsMax Allocation
E (Equity)Nifty 50, equity index fundsHigh12-14%75%
C (Corporate Bonds)AAA-rated corporate bondsModerate9-11%100%
G (Govt Securities)Government bonds, T-billsLow8-10%100%
A (Alternative)REITs, InvITs, CMBSModerate-HighLimited data5%

Active Choice vs Auto Choice — Which Should You Pick?

NPS gives you two ways to allocate your money across asset classes.

Active Choice

You decide the exact percentage allocation across E, C, G, and A. For example, you could choose 75% Equity + 15% Corporate Bonds + 10% Government Securities.

  • Maximum equity (E): 75% until age 50
  • Equity cap reduces: By 2.5% per year after age 50 (reaches 50% at 60)
  • Best for: Investors who understand asset allocation and want maximum equity exposure
  • Recommended allocation for age 25-35: 75% E + 15% C + 5% G + 5% A
  • Recommended allocation for age 45-55: 50% E + 25% C + 20% G + 5% A

Auto Choice (Lifecycle Funds)

PFRDA automatically adjusts your allocation based on your age. As you get older, equity exposure reduces and debt exposure increases. There are three lifecycle options:

LC75 (Aggressive)

  • Starting equity allocation: 75% (until age 35)
  • Equity at 55: Around 15%
  • Best for: Aggressive investors who want the system to auto-rebalance

LC50 (Moderate) — Default Option

  • Starting equity allocation: 50% (until age 35)
  • Equity at 55: Around 10%
  • Best for: Most investors — balanced risk

LC25 (Conservative)

  • Starting equity allocation: 25% (until age 35)
  • Equity at 55: Around 5%
  • Best for: Very conservative investors who prioritise capital safety
FeatureActive ChoiceAuto Choice (LC75)Auto Choice (LC50)Auto Choice (LC25)
You choose allocation?YesNo (auto)No (auto)No (auto)
Max equity at age 3075%75%50%25%
Equity at age 55You decide~15%~10%~5%
Best forKnowledgeable investorsAggressiveMost peopleConservative
RebalancingManual (you change)Automatic yearlyAutomatic yearlyAutomatic yearly

Our recommendation: If you are under 40 and comfortable with equity, go with Active Choice at 75% equity. The extra returns over 20-30 years make a massive difference. If you do not want to think about it, pick Auto Choice LC75.

NPS Fund Manager Comparison (2026)

PFRDA has approved 7 pension fund managers (PFMs) for private sector NPS. You must choose one fund manager for all your asset classes. You can change your fund manager once per year.

Fund ManagerEquity (E) — 10Y ReturnCorp Bond (C) — 10Y ReturnGovt Sec (G) — 10Y ReturnAUM (Rs Crore)Best For
SBI Pension Fund13.5%10.2%9.4%4,50,000+Largest, most trusted
LIC Pension Fund13.1%10.0%9.3%2,80,000+Conservative investors
UTI Retirement Solutions13.3%10.1%9.2%1,60,000+Consistent performer
HDFC Pension Fund13.8%10.4%9.5%1,40,000+Highest equity returns
Kotak Pension Fund13.6%10.3%9.3%90,000+Strong equity track record
Aditya Birla Sun Life PF13.2%10.1%9.1%50,000+Newer entrant, competitive
ICICI Prudential PF13.4%10.2%9.2%70,000+Good across all categories

Key observations:

  • The difference between the best and worst fund managers is only 0.5-0.7% over 10 years. NPS fund management is heavily regulated, so all managers deliver similar returns.
  • HDFC Pension Fund and Kotak Pension Fund have slightly outperformed in equity (E) class.
  • SBI Pension Fund is the largest by AUM and the default choice for most government employees.
  • Expense ratios range from 0.01% to 0.09% — the lowest in the Indian fund management industry. For comparison, an equity mutual fund charges 0.5-1.5%.

Our pick: HDFC Pension Fund or SBI Pension Fund. HDFC for slightly higher equity returns, SBI for the largest track record and trust factor.

NPS Returns History — How Has NPS Performed?

Let us look at the actual numbers. NPS has a solid track record across all asset classes.

Equity (E) Class — 10-Year Performance

  • Average annualised return: 12-14%
  • Best year: 28%+ (2020-21 bull run)
  • Worst year: -8% (2020 COVID crash — recovered within 6 months)
  • Benchmark comparison: NPS Equity has closely tracked the Nifty 50 with slightly lower returns (due to 75% cap and conservative stock selection)

Corporate Bond (C) Class — 10-Year Performance

  • Average annualised return: 9-11%
  • Very stable: No negative year in the last 10 years
  • Comparison: Beats most bank FDs and debt mutual funds

Government Securities (G) Class — 10-Year Performance

  • Average annualised return: 8-10%
  • Safest option: Backed by sovereign guarantee
  • Comparison: Similar to PPF (7.1%) but with market-linked upside

What Rs 10,000/Month in NPS Grows To

Investment PeriodTotal InvestedAt 10% (Conservative)At 12% (Moderate)At 14% (Aggressive)
10 yearsRs 12,00,000Rs 20,48,000Rs 23,00,000Rs 25,93,000
20 yearsRs 24,00,000Rs 75,94,000Rs 98,93,000Rs 1,30,41,000
25 yearsRs 30,00,000Rs 1,32,68,000Rs 1,87,85,000Rs 2,69,41,000
30 yearsRs 36,00,000Rs 2,26,05,000Rs 3,49,50,000Rs 5,49,66,000

At Rs 10,000/month with 12% returns over 30 years, your Rs 36 lakh investment grows to Rs 3.5 crore. Even the 60% lump sum (Rs 2.1 crore) is completely tax-free.

NPS Exit Rules — What Happens When You Retire?

The exit rules are the most misunderstood part of NPS. Here is exactly what happens.

Normal Exit (At Age 60)

  • 60% of corpus: Withdraw as a lump sum — completely tax-free
  • 40% of corpus: Must be used to buy an annuity from a PFRDA-empanelled insurance company
  • Annuity income: Taxed as per your income tax slab
  • You can defer: Withdrawal can be deferred until age 75

What Is an Annuity?

An annuity is a regular monthly pension paid by an insurance company in exchange for a lump sum. When you retire from NPS, you must use 40% of your corpus to buy an annuity. The annuity providers include LIC, SBI Life, HDFC Life, ICICI Prudential, and others.

Annuity rates in India currently range from 6-8% per annum. So if your 40% annuity portion is Rs 40 lakh, you would get approximately Rs 20,000-26,000 per month as pension.

Annuity options available:

  • Pension for life (single) — highest monthly amount
  • Pension for life with return of purchase price to nominee on death
  • Pension for life with pension to spouse after death
  • Pension for life with pension to spouse and then to children

Premature Exit (Before Age 60)

  • Condition: Must have completed at least 5 years in NPS
  • Only 20% can be withdrawn as lump sum
  • 80% must be used to buy an annuity
  • If corpus is less than Rs 2.5 lakh: Entire amount can be withdrawn

Superannuation (Corpus Below Rs 5 Lakh)

If your total NPS corpus at age 60 is below Rs 5 lakh, you can withdraw the entire amount as a lump sum — no annuity purchase required.

Death of Subscriber

The entire corpus (100%) is paid to the nominee. Completely tax-free. No annuity requirement.

NPS vs PPF vs ELSS vs EPF — Which Is Best?

This is the question every Indian investor asks. Here is a comprehensive comparison.

FeatureNPSPPFELSSEPF
Returns (10Y avg)10-14% (asset-dependent)7.1% (fixed by govt)12-15% (market-linked)8.15% (fixed by govt)
RiskLow to High (you choose)Zero (sovereign)High (equity)Zero (sovereign)
Lock-inUntil age 6015 years3 yearsUntil retirement
Tax on investment80C (Rs 1.5L) + 80CCD(1B) (Rs 50K)80C (Rs 1.5L)80C (Rs 1.5L)80C (Rs 1.5L)
Tax on returns60% lump sum tax-free; annuity taxed100% tax-free (EEE)LTCG above Rs 1.25L taxed at 12.5%Interest tax-free (if 5+ years)
LiquidityVery low (partial after 3 years)Low (partial from 7th year)High (after 3 years, fully liquid)Low (partial for specific needs)
Expense ratio0.01-0.09%0%0.5-1.5%0%
Extra tax benefitRs 50,000 under 80CCD(1B)NoneNoneNone
Employer contributionYes (80CCD(2), no cap)NoNoYes (12% of basic)
FlexibilityChoose asset allocation + fund managerNo choiceChoose fundNo choice (limited)
Annuity required?Yes (40% mandatory)NoNoNo
Best forTax saving + retirementSafe long-term savingsTax saving + wealth creationSalaried (mandatory)

The Optimal Strategy for Most Indians

  1. EPF: Continue your mandatory 12% contribution (employer matches it)
  2. NPS: Invest Rs 50,000/year to claim 80CCD(1B) — the extra deduction no other product offers
  3. ELSS or PPF: Use the remaining Rs 1.5 lakh 80C limit based on your risk appetite
  4. Employer NPS (80CCD(2)): Ask your employer to restructure salary to include NPS contribution — this gives you uncapped tax-free benefits

The combination of EPF + NPS + ELSS gives you diversification across risk levels while maximising tax benefits.

How to Open an NPS Account Online (eNPS Portal)

Opening an NPS account takes less than 15 minutes. Here is the step-by-step process.

Method 1: eNPS Portal (Recommended)

  1. Visit enps.nsdl.com (NSDL) or enps.karvy.com (KFintech)
  2. Click "Registration" and select "Individual Subscriber"
  3. Choose account type: Tier 1 (mandatory) — you can add Tier 2 later
  4. Enter your Aadhaar number — OTP verification will be sent to your linked mobile
  5. Fill personal details: Name, date of birth, PAN, bank account, nominee
  6. Choose your investment preferences:
    • Pension Fund Manager (SBI, HDFC, Kotak, etc.)
    • Investment choice (Active or Auto)
    • Asset allocation (if Active choice)
  7. Upload documents: PAN card, photograph, signature (scanned copies)
  8. Make the first contribution: Minimum Rs 500 for Tier 1 (through net banking/UPI/debit card)
  9. Get your PRAN: Permanent Retirement Account Number is generated immediately

Method 2: Through Your Employer (Corporate NPS)

If your company offers NPS as part of the salary structure, the HR department will handle the account opening. You just need to provide your Aadhaar, PAN, and choose your fund manager and allocation.

Method 3: Through Banks and Points of Presence (PoPs)

Visit any NPS Point of Presence — most major banks (SBI, HDFC, ICICI, Axis, Kotak) are PoPs. Carry your Aadhaar, PAN, and a passport-size photo. The bank will help you open the account.

Documents Required

  • Aadhaar card (mandatory for eNPS)
  • PAN card
  • Bank account details (cancelled cheque or passbook)
  • Passport-size photograph
  • Scanned signature

After Opening Your Account

  • Download the NSDL CRA app (available on Android and iOS) to track your NPS investments
  • Set up a standing instruction with your bank for monthly SIP into NPS (minimum Rs 500/month)
  • You can change your fund manager once per year and asset allocation twice per year
  • Keep your PRAN safe — this is your lifetime NPS account number

NPS Charges and Fees

NPS has the lowest charges of any managed investment product in India.

Fee TypeAmount
Account opening (eNPS)Rs 200 (one-time) + Rs 40 PRAN card
Annual maintenanceRs 75/year (CRA charge)
Fund management fee0.01% to 0.09% (the lowest in India)
Transaction chargeRs 3.75 per transaction
Custodian charge0.0032% per annum

For comparison, an equity mutual fund charges 0.5-1.5% as expense ratio. NPS charges less than 0.1%. On a Rs 1 crore corpus, that is a saving of Rs 50,000-1,50,000 per year in fees alone.

Common Mistakes to Avoid with NPS

  1. Choosing low equity allocation when young: If you are 25-35, go with 75% equity. The lock-in period works in your favour — you ride out all the crashes and benefit from compounding.
  2. Not claiming 80CCD(1B): Many investors claim only 80C and miss the extra Rs 50,000 deduction. This is literally leaving Rs 15,600 on the table every year.
  3. Not asking employer for 80CCD(2): If your employer is willing to restructure your salary, the NPS employer contribution gives you unlimited tax-free benefits. Many employees do not even know this option exists.
  4. Investing in NPS under new tax regime without employer contribution: If you are in the new tax regime and your employer does not contribute to NPS, you get zero tax benefit. In this case, direct equity or index funds are better.
  5. Ignoring the annuity component: The 40% mandatory annuity at 6-8% returns is the weakest part of NPS. Plan your other investments (PPF, mutual funds, FDs) to compensate for this.
  6. Not reviewing fund manager performance: While differences are small, check your fund manager's performance annually. You can switch once per year at no cost.

Frequently Asked Questions About NPS

Is NPS better than PPF?

It depends on your goals. NPS offers higher returns (10-14% vs 7.1%) and extra tax benefits (80CCD(1B) — Rs 50,000). But PPF is 100% tax-free at maturity (EEE status) while NPS annuity income is taxed. For pure tax efficiency, PPF wins. For higher returns and the extra Rs 50,000 deduction, NPS wins. The best strategy is to invest in both.

Can I withdraw NPS before 60?

Partial withdrawal is allowed after 3 years for specific reasons (children's education, marriage, house purchase, medical treatment, skill development). You can withdraw up to 25% of your own contributions, maximum 3 times. Full premature exit is allowed after 5 years — but only 20% as lump sum, 80% must buy an annuity.

Is NPS good for the new tax regime?

Only if your employer contributes. Under the new tax regime, you cannot claim 80CCD(1) or 80CCD(1B). But Section 80CCD(2) — employer's contribution — is allowed in BOTH old and new regimes. So if your employer puts 10-14% of your basic into NPS, it is still very beneficial.

What happens to NPS if I change jobs?

Your PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number) stays with you for life. It is not linked to your employer. When you change jobs, you just inform your new employer about your existing PRAN. If the new employer offers corporate NPS, they will contribute to the same account.

Can NRIs invest in NPS?

Yes, NRIs can invest in NPS. However, OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) holders are not eligible. NRI contributions are subject to FEMA regulations, and the account will be closed if the subscriber's citizenship status changes.

The Bottom Line — Should You Invest in NPS?

Yes, if:

  • You are in the 20-30% tax bracket under the old regime and want to save an extra Rs 50,000 in taxes
  • Your employer offers NPS contribution as part of salary (80CCD(2) — works in both tax regimes)
  • You want a low-cost, regulated, long-term retirement product
  • You already max out your 80C limit with PPF/ELSS and want additional deductions

Skip NPS if:

  • You are in the new tax regime without employer NPS contribution
  • You need full liquidity (go with ELSS or direct mutual funds instead)
  • You are already contributing to EPF + PPF and do not need additional retirement allocation

For most Indian salaried employees, the optimal strategy is: EPF (mandatory) + NPS Rs 50,000 (for 80CCD(1B)) + ELSS/PPF (for remaining 80C limit). This combination maximises tax savings while building a diversified retirement corpus across equity, debt, and guaranteed instruments.

Disclaimer: This article is for educational purposes only and does not constitute financial advice. NPS returns are market-linked and subject to change. Tax laws mentioned are as per the Income Tax Act applicable for FY 2025-26. Please consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
excerpt: Complete guide to NPS (National Pension System) for Indian investors in 2026. Covers Tier 1 vs Tier 2 accounts, tax benefits up to Rs 2 lakh under Sections 80CCD(1), 80CCD(1B) and 80CCD(2), asset class allocation, fund manager comparison, historical returns, exit rules, and step-by-step account opening process.

faq.question: Is NPS better than PPF?
faq.answer: NPS offers higher returns (10-14% vs 7.1%) and an extra Rs 50,000 tax deduction under 80CCD(1B) that PPF does not. However, PPF maturity is 100% tax-free (EEE status) while NPS annuity income is taxable. The best approach is to invest in both — Rs 50,000 in NPS for the extra deduction and the rest in PPF for guaranteed, tax-free returns.

faq.question: Can I withdraw from NPS before age 60?
faq.answer: Yes, partial withdrawal of up to 25% of your own contributions is allowed after 3 years for specific reasons — children's education/marriage, house purchase, medical treatment, or skill development. Maximum 3 withdrawals allowed during the entire tenure. Full premature exit is possible after 5 years, but only 20% can be taken as lump sum and 80% must buy an annuity.

faq.question: Does NPS work with the new tax regime?
faq.answer: Partially. You cannot claim 80CCD(1) or 80CCD(1B) deductions in the new tax regime. However, Section 80CCD(2) — your employer's NPS contribution up to 14% of basic salary — is fully tax-exempt in both old and new regimes. If your employer contributes to NPS, it remains a strong benefit even under the new regime.

faq.question: What is the minimum investment in NPS?
faq.answer: For Tier 1, the minimum is Rs 500 per contribution and Rs 1,000 per year to keep the account active. For Tier 2, the minimum is Rs 250 per transaction with no yearly minimum. You can invest via monthly SIP starting from Rs 500/month.

faq.question: How much tax can I save with NPS?
faq.answer: In the 30% tax bracket (old regime), you can save up to Rs 93,600 per year — Rs 46,800 under 80CCD(1) within the Rs 1.5 lakh 80C limit, Rs 15,600 under the additional 80CCD(1B) Rs 50,000 deduction, and Rs 31,200 from employer contribution under 80CCD(2). The exact saving depends on your tax bracket and salary structure.

faq.question: What happens to NPS money when I change jobs?
faq.answer: Your NPS account is portable. Your PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number) stays with you for life regardless of job changes. When you join a new employer, simply share your existing PRAN number. If the new employer offers corporate NPS, contributions continue to the same account.

faq.question: Which NPS fund manager should I choose?
faq.answer: HDFC Pension Fund and SBI Pension Fund are the top performers. HDFC has slightly higher equity returns while SBI has the largest AUM and track record. However, the difference between all 7 fund managers is only 0.5-0.7% over 10 years, so the choice of asset allocation (equity vs debt) matters far more than the fund manager.

faq.question: Is the 40% annuity in NPS a disadvantage?
faq.answer: The mandatory 40% annuity is the most criticised aspect of NPS. Current annuity rates of 6-8% are lower than what you could earn by investing the same amount in mutual funds or FDs. However, annuities provide guaranteed lifelong income, which has value in retirement. Plan your other investments (PPF, mutual funds) to compensate for the lower annuity returns.
